Member-only story

Aiops

Ai Finops

Artificial Intelligence

5 min read
Just now

--

Let me make a specific, falsifiable bet, the kind you can hold me to.
Within the next twelve months, before July 2027, at least one top-tier enterprise software vendor (I’d put my money on Salesforce, ServiceNow, or Microsoft) will announce a flagship product where the default pricing is outcome-based or consumption-based, not per-seat, and will publicly frame per-seat licensing as the legacy model. Not an add-on. Not an experimental SKU buried in the pricing page. The headline default, with per-seat positioned as the old way of doing things.
I want to be precise so this is easy to score in a year. The bet is not “usage-based pricing grows.” It’s that a marquee vendor makes the rhetorical and commercial pivot in public, treating seats as yesterday’s model for a flagship product. If that hasn’t happened by July 2027, I’m wrong, and you can say so.
Here’s why I’m confident enough to put a date on it.
The logic is almost mechanical
Per-seat pricing has a simple premise underneath it. You pay for the number of humans who log in and do work. That premise held for the entire SaaS era because software was a tool that humans operated. The value scaled with the number of people using it, so charging per…
